fund.name: Motilal Oswal Nifty Midcap 150 Index Fund - Direct Plan
fund.fund_house: Motilal Oswal Asset Management Company Limited

allocations.rationale: This low-cost index fund (0.20% yearly fee) tracks India's 150 biggest mid-sized companies — the sweet spot of growth potential and established businesses. Mid-caps tend to grow faster than large caps over long periods. At 27 with 28 years ahead, you can ride the ups and downs. Paired with the S&P 500 fund, you get India + US diversification in just two funds.

fund.name: Motilal Oswal S&P 500 Index Fund - Direct Plan Growth
fund.fund_house: Motilal Oswal Asset Management Company Limited

allocations.rationale: Invests in America's 500 largest companies (Apple, Microsoft, Google etc.) — same 0.20% yearly fee. Adding US stocks means if India slows down, your money isn't entirely dependent on one economy. The rupee also tends to weaken against the dollar over time, giving you an extra boost. This fund is equity-taxed, which is favourable.

fund.name: ICICI Prudential Equity & Debt Fund - Direct Plan - Growth
fund.fund_house: ICICI Prudential Asset Management Company Limited

allocations.rationale: This fund keeps ~65% in stocks and ~35% in safer bonds, automatically reducing your risk. It's equity-taxed (favourable). It acts as your shock absorber — when markets drop, this fund falls less. With your variable freelance income, having some built-in stability is smart. Also useful for your 5-year marriage/travel goal since it's less bumpy.

plan.setup_phase:
Step 1: Park your ₹1 lakh existing corpus in a savings account or RBI floating rate bond fund until you're ready to invest — don't rush.

Step 2: Keep 3 months' expenses (say ₹90,000–₹1.2 lakh) as your emergency fund in a separate savings account. Never invest this.

Step 3: Open a direct mutual fund account on Groww, Kuvera, or Coin (Zerodha). Direct plans save you ~0.5-1% yearly vs regular plans — that's ₹60,000+ over 28 years on just your monthly SIPs.

Step 4: Set up three SIPs: ₹6,000 in the Midcap 150 Index Fund, ₹4,200 in the S&P 500 Index Fund, ₹1,800 in the Equity & Debt Fund. Total: ₹12,000/month. Don't worry if a month's SIP bounces — just resume next month.

Step 5: Invest the remaining ₹1 lakh (after keeping emergency fund) as a one-time lumpsum: ₹50,000 + ₹35,000 + ₹15,000 across the three funds.

Step 6: When you have a good month freelance-wise, just add extra to any of the three funds — no need to overthink proportions. Just invest it.
review_checkpoints: After 6 months: Check that all three SIPs are running smoothly. If any keep bouncing due to variable income, consider switching to a 'manual SIP' — just log in and invest whenever you have the money.
After 1 year: Compare each fund's 1-year return to similar funds. If any has dramatically underperformed (bottom quartile), investigate. Otherwise, do nothing.

After 3 years (marriage/travel goal): Consider shifting the Equity & Debt portion (plus some index fund gains) to a savings account if you'll need it within 1-2 years. Don't sell stocks during a market dip if you can wait.
Every year: Check if your emergency fund still covers 3 months of current expenses. Top it up if your spending has increased.
plan.rebalancing_guidelines: Once a year, check if your allocation has drifted more than 5% from the target (50/35/15). For example, if mid-cap has grown to 55% and S&P 500 has shrunk to 30%, sell some mid-cap and buy more S&P 500. This 'buy low, sell high' happens automatically. Don't rebalance more than once a year — you'll trigger unnecessary taxes and second-guess yourself. Skip rebalancing in years when the drift is under 5%.
plan.rationale: You're 27 with 28 years ahead — that's an incredible runway for compounding. This 3-fund portfolio gives you: (1) India mid-cap growth for higher returns, (2) US large-cap diversification so you're not betting everything on one country, and (3) a hybrid cushion so your portfolio isn't a rollercoaster. All three funds are equity-taxed (favourable), and since you're on the new tax regime, ELSS won't save you any tax — so we skipped it entirely. The two index funds charge just 0.20% yearly, saving you roughly ₹10-15 lakhs vs active funds over your investment horizon. Your variable freelance income is why we keep it simple: just three funds, no rigid rules. Miss a month? No problem. Extra cash? Just add it in.
risks: Stock markets can drop 30-40% in a bad year — your portfolio value will temporarily go down a lot. This is normal and historically recovers over 2-4 years. Don't panic-sell during a crash.
The mid-cap index fund is more volatile than large caps. In a downturn, it falls more. Over 28 years it should recover, but the ride will be bumpier.

The S&P 500 fund is in US dollars — if the rupee strengthens against the dollar, your returns in rupee terms will be lower. Historically the rupee has weakened (~3-4% per year), which helps you, but that may not continue.
As a freelancer, your income can dry up for months. Always keep 3 months' expenses in a savings account before investing. Never invest your emergency fund.
Both index funds have 0.20% fees — far lower than active funds — but they simply track the index. They won't protect you in a crash; they'll fall exactly as much as the market.
plan.disclaimer: For research/educational purposes only. Not certified financial advice.

sip_step_up.description: Increase your monthly SIP by 10% each year as your freelance income grows. After year 1: ₹13,200/month. After year 3: ~₹16,000/month. This single habit can add ₹50+ lakhs over 28 years.

allocation_schedule.trigger: Starting allocation — 28 years to go, full growth mode
15 years in — start gradually reducing risk
5 years before goal — protect what you've built
At retirement — preserve capital, withdraw safely

aps.reasoning:
passive_instrument_fraction: 85% of the equity allocation is in passive index funds (Motilal Oswal Nifty Midcap 150 and S&P 500 Index Fund), while 15% is in an actively managed Aggressive Hybrid fund. Since only equity instruments are considered, and two out of three funds in equity are passive, the score is high but not perfect due to the hybrid fund's active management.

turnover_score: Rebalancing is recommended only once a year and only if allocation drift exceeds 5%. No tactical shifts or frequent trading is suggested. The plan explicitly discourages frequent intervention, indicating a strong buy-and-hold orientation.

cost_emphasis_score: The plan explicitly highlights low expense ratios (0.20%) and compares cost savings over time (e.g., ₹60,000+ saved on SIPs, ₹10–15 lakhs vs active funds). Direct plans are recommended specifically to avoid higher fees, making cost a central theme.

research_vs_cost_score: No individual stock or fund manager analysis is mentioned. Fund selection is based purely on broad market exposure and low cost, not on company fundamentals, management quality, or active research. The rationale relies on diversification and indexing, not security selection.

time_horizon_alignment_score: The plan is designed for a 28-year horizon, with gradual de-risking over decades. Review checkpoints are spaced annually or multi-annually, and the overall tone emphasizes long-term compounding and patience, even during market downturns.

portfolio_activeness_score: Two of the three funds are index funds with no active management intent. The Aggressive Hybrid fund is actively managed but constitutes only 15% of the portfolio. While risk metrics are missing, the category and rationale suggest it provides stability rather than high-conviction active bets. The overall portfolio behaves mostly like a passive, market-exposed portfolio with a small active sleeve for risk mitigation.

pqs.reasoning: The investment plan is well-aligned with Phoebe's profile. She is 27 with a 28-year horizon, moderate risk appetite, variable income, and a preference for simplicity—this 3-fund portfolio delivers exactly that. The 50/35/15 allocation to Indian mid-caps, US large-caps, and an aggressive hybrid fund supports long-term growth while providing diversification and some downside protection. The plan explicitly addresses her financial goals: emergency fund setup, use of the hybrid fund for the 5-year marriage/travel goal, and long-term wealth creation via equities. Diversification is strong across geographies (India + US) and asset classes (mid-cap, large-cap, hybrid), though slightly concentrated in two funds from the same house (Motilal Oswal), but this is minor given their index nature. Risk-return is appropriate: mid-caps are volatile but justified by long horizon; US exposure adds diversification; hybrid fund reduces overall portfolio volatility. The plan is internally consistent—rationale, allocations, rebalancing, and review checkpoints all align. Tax efficiency is excellent: the investor is on the 'new_regime' (no 80C benefit), so ELSS is correctly skipped. All three funds are equity-taxed (Aggressive Hybrid qualifies with ~65% equity), avoiding slab-taxed debt funds—optimal for tax efficiency. The plan explicitly notes this, showing awareness of tax implications. Projected returns (12.25% base) are realistic for this allocation. Rebalancing, SIP flexibility, and step-up guidance match her freelance income pattern. Minor deduction from diversification due to dual Motilal Oswal funds, but not critical. No score deductions elsewhere.
